mnemonics.title: FSTREAM Remember
mnemonics.content: F ile S tream: F = File, S = Stream (flow of data in/out)

mnemonics.title: IF/OF Quick
mnemonics.content: iFstream = F or Reading, OFstream = O ut for Writing

mnemonics.title: SEEK Remember
mnemonics.content: SEEK G = SEE K where G et pointer is, SEEK P = SEE K where P ut pointer is

mnemonics.title: TELL Position
mnemonics.content: T ELL G et position, T ELL P ut position

mnemonics.title: Modes Order
mnemonics.content: Open Modes: I n O ut A ppend B inary - IOAB (sounds like 'yoab')

acronyms.expansion: File Stream (handles both input and output)
acronyms.usage: Use when you need to read AND write to a file
Use when you only need to read data from a file
Use when you only need to write data to a file
Base class containing file open modes (ios::in, ios::out, etc.)
Marker indicating no more data to read
Used with seekg() and seekp() to move file pointers
Three main modes: R (ios::in), W (ios::out), A (ios::app)
tellg()/seekg() for reading, tellp()/seekp() for writing

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a cursor blinking on a line. Reading moves it right, writing inserts at it. Use seekg(n) to jump to character n.
Think of a checkpoint gate (buffer). Data waits there before entering file. flush() opens the gate explicitly.
Arrows: ifstream has arrow pointing INTO program (reading). ofstream has arrow pointing OUT to file (writing).
Think of magnetic tape. tellg() shows current position number, seekg() winds tape to position, read() plays from position.
Checkboxes: □ ios::in □ ios::out □ ios::app □ ios::binary. Check what you need before opening.

analogies.analogy: Like opening a book to a specific page. You need the right mode - 'in' is reading a page, 'out' is writing on a fresh page, 'app' is adding notes at the end.
Like a bus stop (buffer) where passengers (data bytes) wait until enough arrive or the bus leaves (flush/close). Data sits here before going to file.
Like a DVD player. tellg() shows current timestamp, seekg() lets you skip to any timestamp to start playing (reading) from there.
Like reaching the last page of a book. eof() returns true when you've read past the final word, not when you're on it.
Text mode = reading a novel (lines, characters). Binary mode = reading raw code (exact bytes, no translation).
ifstream is like your ears (input to brain), ofstream is like your mouth (output from brain). fstream is both speaking and listening.
